thread.section_title: Mankato, MN Local News and More | NewsBreak

author: NewsBreak Original
text:
By By ANDREW DEZIEL ,
15 days ago
Two weeks after winning re-election comfortably, Waseca Mayor Randy Zimmerman expressed optimism and confidence that the city could enjoy the solid growth, while City Administrator Carl Sonnenberg touted the efficiency of a city government that has learned to do more with less.
As throughout his campaign, Zimmerman told an audience assembled for the Waseca Area Chamber of Commerce’s State of the City address that Waseca stands at a “key crossroads,” well positioned to achieve growth but only with wise decisions and investments.
“The iron is hot,” Zimmerman said. “Partnerships are being formed, relationships are being rekindled, and the spirit of teamwork is in full steam. “We have a beautiful community that simply needs a little nudge, a spark, to set off an exciting time for growth and revitalization.”
While discontent with the status quo may have led to a change at the top of the ticket, Waseca residents appeared pleased with their local officials and stayed the course, voting to re-elect not only Zimmerman but Councilors Mark Christiansen and Stacey Schroeder.
Over the coming year, Zimmerman promised that he alongside the Council would build on their efforts to ensure that the future is bright and Waseca’s residents and small business owners have the confidence they need to take risks and invest in their community.
“I firmly believe we can lead Waseca towards a very efficient, safe, affordable and bustling city once again, filled with many opportunities,” Zimemrman said.
Key to that will be continued work with state and federal officials to enhance Waseca’s infrastructure, especially ongoing efforts to upgrade a stormwater system which has been repeatedly overwhelmed in recent years, requiring the discharge of excess water.
Expanding housing is also a priority for Zimmerman and the Council, who see addressing the current housing shortage as essential to attracting business investment. In 2025, Zimmerman said a new housing development and apartment building are on track to launch.
The two badly needed additions to a housing market that is roughly 300 units short of meeting demand, according to a city study, are likely to help the city as it is currently courting several businesses, including ones that could help the city revitalize its historic downtown.
On top of all that, Zimmerman promised that he and the Council would work to boost the quality of life, strengthening the city’s robust public safety departments and increasing opportunities for entertainment and recreation.
Zimmerman also showered praise on City Manager Carl Sonnenberg for his wise leadership, encyclopedic knowledge of city government and carefully calibrated efforts to foster growth while lowering taxes and maintaining high quality city services.
A city hall staple for a whopping 44 years, Sonnenberg said he is grateful to be able to serve the city and work alongside many outstanding co-workers. Over the last four years, he’s worked to find budget cuts to meet the city’s budget goals, largely by trimming back on personnel.
Today, the city’s workforce is actually one person smaller than it was 20 years ago. Resources have been shifted away from city hall, public works and the parks department and towards Waseca’s police and fire departments.
Sonnenberg has cautioned against further cuts to the city’s personnel budget, arguing that drops in the quality of service would be likely to follow, and this year’s budget, while not yet final, is expected to include an increase after several years of levy decreases.
In October, the Council approved a preliminary 4.4% levy increase, avoiding major cuts to future city infrastructure projects while also keeping the tax rate below 75%. The Council could yet opt to reduce the levy when it passes a final budget in December, but can’t vote to increase it.
Even with current low levels of staffing, the city has worked to improve its parks and infrastructure, authorizing the establishment of a new dog park, investing in Clear Lake Park and adopting a trespass policy to reduce vandalism in city parks.
Waseca’s public safety departments benefited from investment to maintain a high quality of service, maintaining an above average clearance rate of crimes and aggressively responding to even the most challenging of fires, like a grass and wildland fire that burnt over 2,000 acres.
The city has kept up with its Capital Improvement Plan, investing both in trucks and equipment for public safety and other departments, and in a series of street, sidewalk and trail and water infrastructure projects as well as upgrades to the municipal airport.
In the coming year, the city will continue to seek grants to help improve local infrastructure, reduce I&I (inflow and infiltration) in the city’s sewer system, all while grappling with increasingly expensive unfunded mandates and post-COVID cost increases.
Potential annexation is also sure to be a topic of discussion, along with the Gaiter Lake project and Sonnenberg’s proposal to eliminate street assessments. Councilor Schroeder said the Council should be looking to annex additional areas urgently, while Zimmerman emphasized the importance of better developing areas currently within city limits.
“We have existing properties within city limits which could easily be improved exponentially,” Zimmerman said. “Whether it’s our downtown area or a couple of parcels which scream for residential development, but the process is you have to have willing participants on the part of the landowners.”
